section.title: Warning Against Idolatry
section.sectionRationale: This chapter addresses internal threats to covenant loyalty, a prophet, a family member, or an entire city enticing Israel to idolatry, prescribing severe measures bound tightly to Israel's unique covenant nationhood that must never be read as warrant for violence against dissenting belief in any later era.
section.thesis: Moses warns that even a prophet with real signs, a beloved family member, or an entire city can become an instrument of enticement to idolatry, and Israel must love the LORD supremely enough to reject and purge such enticement decisively, however painful, though these commands are bound to Israel's unique role as a covenant nation and are never a warrant for violence against dissenting belief today.
section.frameNote: Three movements: false prophets who lead astray, even with real signs; enticement from a beloved family member or closest friend; and an entire city that has gone astray after careful investigation.

verses.text: If a {{nabi|prophet}} or dreamer of dreams arises among you and proclaims a sign or wonder to you,
and if the sign or wonder he has spoken to you comes about, but he says, “Let us follow other gods (which you have not known) and let us worship them,”
you must not listen to the words of that prophet or dreamer. For the LORD your God is {{nasah|testing}} you to find out whether you love Him with all your heart and with all your soul.
You are to follow the LORD your God and fear Him. Keep His commandments and listen to His voice; serve Him and hold fast to Him.

Such a prophet or dreamer must be put to death, because he has advocated rebellion against the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt and redeemed you from the house of slavery; he has tried to turn you from the way in which the LORD your God has commanded you to walk. So you must purge the evil from among you.

units.label: Even a brother or closest friend

verses.text: If your very own brother, or your son or daughter, or the wife you embrace, or your closest friend secretly entices you, saying, “Let us go and worship other gods” (which neither you nor your fathers have known,
the gods of the peoples around you, whether near or far, whether from one end of the earth or the other),
you must not yield to him or listen to him. Show him no pity, and do not spare him or shield him.
Instead, you must surely kill him. Your hand must be the first against him to put him to death, and then the hands of all the people.
Stone him to death for trying to turn you away from the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.
Then all Israel will hear and be afraid, and will never again do such a wicked thing among you.
If, regarding one of the cities the LORD your God is giving you to inhabit, you hear it said
that wicked men have arisen from among you and have led the people of their city astray, saying, “Let us go and serve other gods” (which you have not known),
then you must inquire, investigate, and interrogate thoroughly. And if it is established with certainty that this abomination has been committed among you,
you must surely put the inhabitants of that city to the sword. {{cherem|Devote to destruction}} all its people and livestock.

And you are to gather all its plunder in the middle of the public square, and completely burn the city and all its plunder as a whole burnt offering to the LORD your God. The city must remain a mound of ruins forever, never to be rebuilt.
Nothing devoted to destruction shall cling to your hands, so that the LORD will turn from His fierce anger, grant you mercy, show you compassion, and multiply you as He swore to your fathers,
because you obey the LORD your God, keeping all His commandments I am giving you today and doing what is right in the eyes of the LORD your God.

nabi.gloss: Prophet, one who speaks on behalf of God. This chapter warns that the office and even accurate signs do not authenticate a message; a prophet's words must always be tested against what the LORD has already revealed.

nasah.gloss: To test, to try, to prove. Here God Himself is described as permitting a test through a false prophet's fulfilled sign, exposing whether Israel's love for Him is genuine and supreme above every other claim on their loyalty.

cherem.gloss: To devote irrevocably to destruction. Applied here to an apostate Israelite city, showing that the severity once reserved for Canaanite idolatry in chapter 7 applies equally within Israel itself when covenant loyalty is abandoned for other gods.

basic.context: Moses warns Israel that enticement to idolatry can come from surprising and painful sources, a prophet performing real signs, a beloved family member, or an entire city, and commands that such enticement be rejected and purged decisively rather than tolerated because of its source's credibility or closeness.
basic.hermeneutics: This is one of Deuteronomy's hardest passages and needs honest, careful reading: it prescribes capital measures bound tightly to Israel's unique identity as a covenant nation-state, where loyalty to the LORD was inseparable from national and civil identity. It gives no warrant whatsoever for the church or any government to use violence against religious dissent or unbelief today.
basic.originalLanguages: Even a genuine nabi, prophet, whose sign comes true is to be rejected if his message contradicts the LORD; the fulfilled sign itself functions as a nasah, test, of whether Israel's love for God is supreme. An apostate city is subject to cherem, the same total devotion to destruction earlier commanded against Canaanite idolatry, now applied within Israel itself.
basic.typology: Jesus warns that false prophets and false messiahs will perform great signs and wonders to deceive even the elect if possible, Matthew 24:24, directly echoing this chapter's principle that a real sign does not validate a message contrary to God's word.
basic.christ: Where Israel's civil order used the sword to purge enticement to idolatry, the New Testament calls the church to purge evil from its fellowship through spiritual discipline, not violence, 1 Corinthians 5:13, and entrusts final judgment on all deception entirely to Christ at His return, 2 Thessalonians 2:8-10.
basic.god: God is jealous for exclusive, supreme love from His people, so much so that He allows even a false prophet's fulfilled sign to function as a genuine test of that love; yet the chapter closes by naming mercy, not mere severity, as God's ultimate purpose for His people's obedience.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 24:24 warns of false messiahs and prophets performing great signs to deceive. 2 Thessalonians 2:9-10 describes the lawless one's false wonders. 1 Corinthians 5:13 calls the church to purge evil from its own fellowship through discipline, not violence. 2 Thessalonians 1:6-10 entrusts final judgment on evil entirely to Christ.
basic.oneStory: This chapter names how seriously God takes the exclusivity of His people's love, even when enticement comes from credible or beloved sources. The New Testament carries this same seriousness forward into the church's call to discern truth, exercise loving discipline, and trust final judgment on all deception entirely to Christ.

context.literary: Following directly from the centralization of worship in chapter 12, this chapter addresses threats to that exclusive worship arising from within Israel itself, moving from the most public source of enticement, a prophet, to the most intimate, family, to the most communal, an entire city.
context.historical: Prophetic figures, sign-workers, and local civic loyalty all carried significant social weight in the ancient world; this chapter directly confronts the temptation to trust such credibility or closeness over fidelity to the LORD's already-revealed word.
context.canonical: This chapter's concern about false signs deceiving God's people becomes a recurring New Testament warning, from Jesus' Olivet discourse to Paul's description of the lawless one's counterfeit wonders, showing this danger persists in every era even as the response changes under the new covenant.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ensure Israel's exclusive loyalty to the LORD could not be undermined by the credibility of a sign-working prophet, the intimacy of family loyalty, or the momentum of communal apostasy, treating all three with equal seriousness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific civil and capital penalties prescribed here belong to Israel's unique covenant nationhood, in which religious and civil identity were inseparable; they are not prescriptive for the church or any modern state, which hold no equivalent theocratic mandate. What is prescriptive and abiding is the call to test every claim, however credible or beloved its source, against God's already-revealed word, and to take seriously the danger of enticement to false worship.

debates.question: How should Christians read the death penalty prescribed here for enticing others to idolatry, even applied to family members or entire cities?

views.label: Bound to Israel's unique theocratic covenant
views.case: Israel functioned uniquely as a covenant nation-state in which loyalty to the LORD was simultaneously religious and civil identity; these penalties belong to that specific national administration and are never extended to the church or any modern state, which wields no equivalent theocratic mandate, John 18:36, 2 Corinthians 10:3-4.

views.label: An enduring picture of the seriousness of apostasy, not a transferable legal precedent
views.case: Some read this passage primarily as urgent, vivid language underscoring how seriously God takes covenant betrayal, using the strongest sanctions available within Israel's specific civil order, without intending these particular penalties as a pattern for any other era or nation.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree this text gives no warrant whatsoever for the church or any government to inflict violence for religious dissent or unbelief today; the New Testament redirects God's people to persuasion, loving discipline within the church, and final judgment entrusted entirely to Christ, 2 Thessalonians 1:6-10.

study.originalLanguages: Even a nabi, prophet, whose sign comes true is rejected here if his message pulls toward other gods, because the fulfilled sign itself becomes God's nasah, test, of whether Israel's love is truly supreme. Communal apostasy is met with cherem, the same devoted destruction earlier commanded against Canaanite idolatry, now shockingly turned inward if Israel itself abandons the LORD.

typology.type: A prophet's real sign used to entice toward false worship
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Jesus' warning that false messiahs and prophets will perform great signs to deceive even the elect if possible, Matthew 24:24.

typology.type: Purging evil decisively from the community
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the church's call to purge evil through loving spiritual discipline, not violence, 1 Corinthians 5:13.

study.christ: Jesus explicitly warns that false messiahs and false prophets will arise performing great signs and wonders to deceive even the elect if possible, Matthew 24:24, directly echoing this chapter's principle that a genuine sign never validates a message contrary to God's revealed word. Whereas Israel's civil order used the sword to purge such enticement, the New Testament calls the church to purge evil from its own fellowship through spiritual discipline, 1 Corinthians 5:13, entrusting final judgment on all deception entirely to Christ at His return, 2 Thessalonians 2:8-10.
study.god: God is jealous for the exclusive, supreme love of His people, so much so that He allows even a false prophet's fulfilled sign to function as a genuine test of whether that love is real; yet this chapter closes not with severity as the final word but with mercy, verse 17, as God's ultimate purpose for calling His people to obedience.

crossReferences.note: Jesus warns that false messiahs and prophets will perform great signs to deceive even the elect if possible.
Describes the lawless one's counterfeit signs and wonders deceiving those who refuse to love the truth.
Calls the church to purge evil from its own fellowship through discipline, never through violence.
Entrusts final judgment on all evil and deception entirely to Christ at His return.

study.oneStory: This chapter names how seriously God takes the exclusivity of His people's love, even when enticement comes from credible signs, beloved family, or communal pressure. The New Testament carries this same seriousness forward, calling the church to test every claim against God's word, exercise loving discipline within its own fellowship, and trust final judgment on all deception entirely to Christ.

quiz.q: According to verse 3, why might the LORD allow a false prophet's sign or wonder to actually come true?

options.text: To test whether Israel truly loves the LORD with all their heart and soul.
options.note: Verse 3 gives this exact reason for God permitting such a test.

options.text: Because false prophets always have real power equal to God's.
options.note: The text frames this as a test God permits, not evidence of equal or rival power.

options.text: It never actually happens; false signs never come true.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly considers the case where the sign does come about.

options.text: No reason is given.
options.note: Verse 3 gives an explicit theological reason.

quiz.q: What does Moses say should happen if a close family member secretly entices someone to worship other gods (vv.6-9)?

options.text: They must not yield or show pity, and must be the first to put that person to death, without shielding them.
options.note: Verses 8-9 state this severe requirement explicitly.

options.text: They should be reported to the elders for a fine.
options.note: The text prescribes a far more severe response than a fine, capital punishment carried out by the community.

options.text: They should be forgiven immediately without any consequence.
options.note: The passage explicitly forbids showing pity or sparing the person, the opposite of automatic forgiveness.

options.text: Nothing is instructed regarding family members.
options.note: Verses 6-9 address this situation directly and at length.

quiz.q: What must happen before an entire city can be judged for turning to idolatry (v.14)?

options.text: Israel must inquire, investigate, and interrogate thoroughly to establish with certainty that the report is true.
options.note: Verse 14 requires exactly this careful process of verification.

options.text: A single unverified rumor is sufficient for immediate judgment.
options.note: The text requires thorough investigation, the opposite of acting on unverified rumor.

options.text: No verification process is needed at all.
options.note: Verse 14 explicitly requires careful investigation before any judgment.

options.text: Nothing about investigation is mentioned.
options.note: Verse 14 addresses this process explicitly.

quiz.q: What happens to a whole city confirmed to have turned to other gods (vv.15-16)?

options.text: Its people and livestock are devoted to destruction and the city is burned completely, never to be rebuilt.
options.note: Verses 15-16 describe this severe, total judgment.

options.text: Only its leaders would be punished.
options.note: The judgment described extends to the whole city, not only its leaders.

options.text: The city would simply be fined heavily.
options.note: The judgment described is total destruction, far more severe than a fine.

options.text: No punishment for the city is described.
options.note: Verses 15-16 describe the punishment in explicit detail.

quiz.q: What is the ultimate purpose given for these severe measures against enticement to idolatry (v.18)?

options.text: So that Israel would obey the LORD, keep His commandments, and do what is right, and He would show them mercy.
options.note: Verses 17-18 name mercy and covenant faithfulness as the ultimate purpose.

options.text: So that Israel could seize more territory and wealth.
options.note: No such purpose is named; the stated goal is obedience leading to God's mercy.

options.text: So that other nations would fear and worship Israel instead of God.
options.note: The purpose named is Israel's own obedience and God's mercy toward them, not the nations' worship of Israel.

options.text: No purpose is given for these measures.
options.note: Verses 17-18 give an explicit purpose.

questions: Moses warns that even someone with real spiritual gifts or apparent signs can lead people away from God if their message contradicts His revealed word. How do you currently test what you hear, even from gifted or persuasive people, against Scripture itself?

This chapter names even a beloved family member as a potential source of enticement away from God. Is there a relationship in your life where love has quietly made you unwilling to name a harmful spiritual influence honestly?

This passage belongs to Israel's unique covenant nationhood and gives no warrant for violence toward anyone today; instead the church is called to loving discipline, trusting final judgment to Christ. Where do you need to release judgment of someone into God's hands rather than trying to control or punish them yourself?

Before judging an entire city, Israel was required to investigate thoroughly rather than act on rumor. Where might you be tempted to judge a situation quickly rather than seeking out the full truth first?

The purpose behind these hard commands was ultimately mercy, obedience leading to God's compassion (v.18). How does viewing even God's hardest commands as ultimately aimed at mercy change how you read a passage like this one?

translationNotes.note: This passage's logic, that God may permit a real sign to accompany a false message as a test of love, is echoed directly by Jesus in Matthew 24:24 regarding false messiahs and prophets in the last days.

'Purge the evil from among you' is a recurring formula throughout Deuteronomy's legal sections, marking the seriousness with which covenant-breaking sin was to be treated within Israel's specific civil order.

'A whole burnt offering' likens the apostate city's total destruction to a sacrificial offering, framing this severe judgment within Israel's own worship categories rather than as an act of mere warfare.
